mission.title: Install and Configure MySQL Operator for Kubernetes (Oracle)
mission.description: The MySQL Operator for Kubernetes automates the management of MySQL InnoDB Cluster setups within a Kubernetes environment. It simplifies tasks such as deployment, scaling, and backup, making it ideal for teams looking to streamline database operations. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the MySQL Operator Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install the operator using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add mysql-operator https://mysql.github.io/mysql-operator/

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the official MySQL Operator Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install MySQL Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the MySQL Operator using Helm, creating the necessary namespace and deploying the operator.

```bash

helm install mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --create-namespace --version 9.0.0

```

> Installs the MySQL Operator with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify Operator Deployment
steps.description:
After installation, this step checks if the MySQL Operator is running correctly in the specified namespace.

```bash

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> This command retrieves the deployment status of the MySQL Operator.

steps.title: Create MySQL Root User Secret
steps.description:
This step creates a Kubernetes secret that contains the credentials for the MySQL root user, which is necessary for managing the MySQL InnoDB Cluster.

```bash

kubectl create secret generic mypwds --from-literal=rootUser=root --from-literal=rootHost=% --from-literal=rootPassword: <REDACTED>'

```

> Creates a secret named 'mypwds' with MySQL root credentials.

steps.title: Define MySQL InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
This step defines a MySQL InnoDB Cluster resource in Kubernetes, referencing the previously created secret.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.26'
EOF

```

> Creates an InnoDB Cluster named 'mycluster' using the secret.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.26'
EOF

steps.title: Verify InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
This step checks if the MySQL InnoDB Cluster has been created successfully and is running.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator

```

> Retrieves the status of InnoDB Clusters in the mysql-operator namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall MySQL Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MySQL Operator and its associated resources from the cluster. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if not backed up.

```bash

helm uninstall mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl delete namespace mysql-operator

```

> Uninstalls the MySQL Operator from the specified namespace.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade MySQL Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your MySQL data. This step performs the upgrade and verifies the deployment afterward.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator -o json > backup_innodbclusters.json

helm upgrade mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --version 9.0.1

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> Backs up the current InnoDB Cluster configuration.
> Upgrades the MySQL Operator to the specified version.
> Verifies the deployment status after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator -o json > backup_innodbclusters.json

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create InnoDB Cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the secret containing the MySQL root credentials is missing or incorrect.

**Fix:**
Ensure the secret 'mypwds' exists and contains the correct credentials.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'MySQL Operator is not running'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The MySQL Operator deployment may have failed due to insufficient permissions or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the deployment logs with 'kubectl logs deployment/mysql-operator -n mysql-operator' for errors.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'InnoDB Cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the MySQL instances are not able to communicate with each other.

**Fix:**
Verify the network policies and service configurations for the MySQL instances.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to MySQL'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may indicate that the MySQL service is not exposed correctly or the credentials are wrong.

**Fix:**
Check the service configuration and ensure the correct credentials are being used.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for MySQL 8.0.26, improved backup and restore functionalities.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 8.4, ensure that your InnoDB Cluster configurations are compatible with the new version.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator' showing the status of your InnoDB Cluster as 'Ready'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.26'
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster set up and accessible.
